title: Non-Local and Non-Markovian Effects of a Microscopic Two-Level Defect in Superconducting Quantum Circuits
authors: Yang Gao, Yujia Zhang, Huikai Xu, Pan Shi, Feiyu Li, Yaqing Feng, Weijie Sun, Jiayu Ding, Yang Liu, He Wang, Ruixia Wang, Zhen Yang, Yirong Jin, Haifeng Yu, Fei Yan
abstract: Microscopic two-level systems (TLS) -- ubiquitous atomic-scale defects in solid-state quantum devices -- are a dominant source of qubit decoherence, yet their role is often considered local and short-memoried. Here, we report the observation of a coherent TLS that couples simultaneously to two spatially distant superconducting qubits. The TLS is identified to reside within the tunable coupler linking the qubits, enabling controllability of the TLS-qubit coupling strength via coupler frequency -- a capability absent in earlier studies. This tunability allows us to systematically probe how TLS distorts qubit dynamics, revisiting the decoherence model in the presence of non-Markovian TLS dephasing noise. This is corroborated by the reconstructed $1/f$ noise spectrum of TLS frequency fluctuation spanning more than ten orders of magnitude (0.1\,mHz -- 1\,MHz) that reveals discrete fluctuator signatures. Quantum process tomography further unveils TLS-induced correlated qubit dynamics, highlighting the long-lived TLS as an effective source of non-Markovianity. Our findings expose a previously overlooked interaction mechanism in scalable quantum architectures: defects embedded in coupling elements can simultaneously affect multiple qubits with variable impact. Beyond immediate implications for system characterization and calibration, this situation provides a powerful testbed for studying defect-driven quantum dynamics, refining error suppression strategies, and advancing architecture design for scalable quantum technologies.
summary: The study reveals that a single microscopic defect in a superconducting coupler can simultaneously affect multiple distant qubits. By tuning the coupler, the researchers controlled the defect's impact, uncovering non-local correlated errors and non-Markovian dynamics. This finding is crucial for designing scalable quantum computers where defects in coupling elements can act as shared error sources.
main_problem: Investigating how a single microscopic two-level system (TLS) embedded in a tunable coupler induces non-local, non-Markovian, and correlated decoherence in spatially separated superconducting qubits.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated a shared TLS that couples to two distant qubits, showing that its impact can be tuned via the coupler frequency, leading to non-Markovian dephasing and correlated qubit dynamics.
method: The study uses spectroscopy, time-domain iSWAP operations, noise spectroscopy (repeated Ramsey and spin-locking), and quantum process tomography on a 72-qubit superconducting processor.
model_or_system: A superconducting quantum processor featuring two frequency-tunable transmon qubits connected via a frequency-tunable transmon coupler, with a single TLS located within the coupler's Josephson junction tunnel barrier.
key_observables: TLS frequency, coupling strengths (g1, g2, gT), coherence times (T1, T2), noise power spectral density (PSD), qubit purity, and concurrence.
important_parameters: Dispersive regime (large detuning), TLS frequency (~3.48 GHz), noise spectrum spanning 0.1 mHz to 1 MHz, and the Zeno/anti-Zeno regimes of TLS dissipation.
main_assumptions: The TLS is assumed to be located within the coupler's junction barrier and follows a 1/f-like noise profile modeled by independent two-level fluctuators.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 3 displays the TLS dephasing noise power spectral density; Figure 4 illustrates the pulse sequence for measuring concurrence and purity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of TLS-induced decoherence, describes the experimental platform and TLS identification, details the measurement of non-local and tunable coupling, presents noise spectroscopy results, and concludes with implications for scalable architecture design.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for open quantum systems researchers as it provides an experimental realization of non-Markovian, non-local noise and explores the transition between Zeno and anti-Zeno regimes in a multi-qubit architecture.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Characterization of a microscopic defect residing in a tunable coupler. a, Experimental setup: two transmon qubits (separated by more than 1 mm in physical distance) coupled via a frequency-tunable transmon coupler hosting a TLS within a Josephson junction. Effective TLS-qubit couplings (˜g1, ˜g2) arise from interactions mediated by the coupler (gT: TLS-coupler; g1,g2: qubit-coupler). Microwave pulses applied to the coupler directly drive the TLS via the cross-resonance effect. b, Verification of the shared TLS: sequential iSWAP operations transferring an excitation from Q1 to TLS to Q2 by tuning the qubits into resonance with the TLS. Maximum Q2 excitation is achieved when pulse...

FIG. 2. Coupler-assisted TLS-qubit coupling. a, Spectroscopy of Q1 versus flux bias (pulse sequence illustrated above) for varying coupler-qubit detunings ∆while Q2 is biased far away from the relevant region. Avoided crossings in the spectrum (top to bottom) demonstrate enhanced ˜g1 as ∆decreases, accompanied by TLS frequency shift due to level repulsion from the coupler. The corresponding level diagram is illustrated in the inset in the top center. b, Qubit dynamics under the T1 relaxation protocol for varying qubit frequencies near the TLS resonance (pulse sequence illustrated above). Increased coupling accelerates qubit-TLS energy exchange and broadens the spectral interaction range...

FIG. 3. Power spectral density of the TLS dephasing noise. Multi-colored lines: noise PSD (0.1 mHz–10 kHz) derived from repeated Ramsey experiments of various sampling rates (1/ts). Purple dots: noise PSD (∼1 MHz) derived from spin- locking experiments. Red dashed line: added-up noise spectrum of several independent TLFs, with each Lorentzian spectrum plotted in black lines. Shown in the insets are the repeated-Ramsey and spin-locking protocols. FIG. 4. Correlated qubit dynamics induced by TLS. a, Pulse sequence to measure the concurrence and purity of a two-qubit system. First tuned the coupler to decouple the qubits, then qubits Q1 and Q2 are prepared in one of the initial states: |00⟩, |01⟩, |10⟩, or |11⟩. The qubits are next brought into resonance with TLS, δ represents the qubit frequency detuning from the TLS. After a variable delay, the two-qubit density matrix ρ is measured using state tomography. b, Purity and c, Concurrence derived from state tomography.
